summary:
The Congo Free State (1885–1908) was the personal property of Belgian King Leopold II rather than a Belgian colony — a private state covering approximately 2.3 million square kilometers of Central Africa, roughly 76 times the size of Belgium, controlled by Leopold under the guise of humanitarian and 'civilizing' mission. It became the site of some of the worst atrocities in colonial history, with a system of terror and forced labor that killed millions of Congolese people and triggered the first major international human rights campaign.

Leopold had secured international recognition for his control of the Congo at the [[event:2546|Berlin Conference]] of 1884–85 by presenting his International Association of the Congo as a humanitarian enterprise dedicated to fighting slavery and opening the region to free trade. In reality, once in control, Leopold organized the Congo as a massive extraction enterprise focused on wild rubber and ivory. To meet production quotas, the Force Publique (the colonial army staffed by African soldiers under Belgian officers) terrorized the population into working: hostage-taking, flogging, burning villages, and the notorious practice of cutting off the hands of those who failed to meet their quotas or whose bullets were not accounted for.

The scale of the atrocity gradually became known through the work of missionaries, journalists, and the Congo Reform Association led by British journalist E.D. Morel and former diplomat Roger Casement, whose 1904 report to the British Foreign Office provided systematic documentation of the abuses. The international outcry was eventually sufficient to force Leopold to transfer the Congo to Belgian state control in 1908. Estimates of excess deaths during Leopold's regime range from several hundred thousand to as many as 10 million — the exact toll remains contested but was catastrophic.

The Congo atrocities had a significant cultural impact. Joseph Conrad's novella Heart of Darkness (1899), drawing on his own observations from a journey up the Congo River in 1890, portrayed the horror of colonial exploitation in terms that remain among the most powerful in literature. The Congo reform campaign itself established a template for international human rights advocacy that influenced twentieth-century humanitarian movements.
